participants.reasoning: The extracted participants match the official criteria, randomization, and evaluated sample details, with only minor paraphrasing and no substantive discrepancies.
participants.extracted: The trial enrolled 96 participants from 168 assessed for eligibility at a single center in India. Eligible participants were boys and girls aged 11 to 17 years who required fixed orthodontic treatment, had moderate-to-severe mandibular anterior crowding (4–9 mm) that still allowed bracket engagement, had eruption of all mandibular anterior teeth, had no medical problems or medications affecting pain perception, and provided informed consent (with parental/guardian consent for minors). Exclusions included severe deep bite, need for treatment procedures beyond continuous archwire mechanics, chronic pain medication use, prior dental or orofacial pain, and medical conditions precluding fixed appliance use (such as nickel allergy or recent seizures). After one lost to follow-up and 10 exclusions from analysis due to bond failure or incomplete questionnaires, 85 participants remained for analysis (42 males, 43 females; mean age 14.1 ± 2.0 years).
participants.official:
Inclusion criteria
Age 11–17 years
Need for fixed orthodontic treatment
Moderate–severe crowding (4‐9 mm) in the mandibular anterior segment that was not severe enough to prevent bracket engagement (patients with severe crowding related to 1 or 2 teeth (such as blocked out lateral incisors) were not included)
Eruption of all mandibular anterior teeth
No history of medical problems/medication that could influence pain perception
Informed and witnessed consent from the minor participant and their

interventions.reasoning: The extracted interventions match the official comparison of 0.016-inch superelastic NiTi versus 0.0175-inch multistranded stainless steel archwires used with the same fixed appliance setup.
interventions.extracted: The study compared two orthodontic initial archwire interventions used after bonding fixed appliances to the mandibular arch. One intervention was a 0.016-inch superelastic nickel–titanium archwire (austenitic active, preformed ovoid, superelastic arch wire; 3M Unitek Corporation), and the other was a 0.0175-inch multistranded stainless steel archwire (six-stranded coaxial wire; Unitek Coaxial Wire; 3M Unitek Corporation).
interventions.official:
Comparison: multistrand StSt vs superelastic NiTi
Group A:0.0175‐inch multistrand StSt (6‐stranded, Unitek Coaxial Wire; 3M Unitek)
Group B:0.016‐inch superelastic NiTi (austenitic active, preformed ovoid; 3M Unitek)
Other interventions:Preadjusted Edgewise Appliances with 0.022 × 0.028‐inch slot twin brackets (Roth prescription, Gemini Metal Brackets; 3M Unitek) were bonded directly to the mandibular dentition using light‐cure composite resin (Transbond XT, 3M Unitek).
Brackets:0.022 × 0.028‐in

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes describe baseline and 32 follow-up time points over 14 days with mixed-model longitudinal analysis, which does not match the official pain assessment schedule of 1, 2, 4, 6, and 12 hours then daily timings through day 14.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure orthodontic pain as the main outcome using the Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), a 100-mm horizontal line anchored by ‘no pain’ and ‘worst pain possible’. Pain was assessed at baseline and at 32 prespecified follow-up time points after initial archwire placement over a 14-day period. The protocol specified assessment at the rest position only, with participants recording their perceived pain at each time point. A second, related outcome framework was the repeated longitudinal pain profile across all post-placement time points, analyzed as mean VAS scores over time using mixed-model methods.
outcomes.official:
Primary outcomes:the study reported neither of our primary outcomes
Secondary outcomes
Pain:intensity of pain, measured on a 100‐mm VAS, at 1, 2, 4, 6, and 12 hours for first 12 hours and then 3 times (morning, afternoon, and bedtime) daily on days 1–7, day 10, and day 14
